Current Genomic and Computational Progress

Progress in functional genomic and other experimental methods

Here, we list four developments that have allowed the Alpha Project to be contemplated. First, the ability to generate, express, purify and work with affinity tagged proteins has vastly increased the ability of scientists to make quantitative measurements in vivo and in vitro (Ausubel et al., 1987-2001, chapters 10,11 and 16). Second, the development of fluorescent proteins from Aequorea victoria and other organisms (see Chalfie et al., 1998; Tsien, 1998) has enabled new kinds of non-invasive single-cell measurements. Third, the ability to select (in vitro and by two-hybrid) high quality affinity reagents from combinatorial libraries, including single chain antibodies and our own peptide aptamers, has made it feasible to consider selecting such reagents specific to each native and modified member of the pathway. Fourth, improvements in protein mass spectrometry, including gentle ionization methods and the ability to resolve large numbers of low-abundance mass peaks with extraordinary resolution via Fourier Transform Ion Cyclotron Resonance, have made it possible to use mass readouts to enumerate and quantify expressed protein complements. Thus allowing us to use changes in mass to quantify kinetic events in phosphorylation-based signaling pathways.Progress in computing the behavior of biological systems

Physically grounded methods for representing the time progression of systems are constrained by time and length scales. For example, molecular dynamic simulations of an ion channel can represent the behavior of individual atoms over 10ns (Sansome et al, 2000), while models of ocean and atmosphere physics can represent the conditions in 100km2 areas over months and years (Lynch et al, 1998). Our bias (at first) and our experience (now) has led us to model intracellular events at the level of molecular species and their reactions with one another (Endy et al. 2000; Endy & Brent, 2001). Typically, the quantitative time dependent behavior of these models is computed using simulations comprised of coupled differential equations solved numerically by stochastic kinetic methods (Gillespie, 1976; Arkin & McAdams, 1998; Gibson & Bruck, 2000; Endy, unpublished). The output of such simulations varies from run to run, so that the prediction of average behavior requires many runs. The major benefit from building simulations that capture species, reactions, and fluctuations is that this level of abstraction matches the current level of biological understanding and colloquy ? experiments that perturb specific molecular species, and the interpretation of such experiments, remain the mainstay of contemporary molecular and cellular biology. Thus, we can realistically plan to develop and apply simulations that are tightly coupled to our experimental efforts. However, there are significant difficulties in developing nontrivial simulations at this level of resolution. First, such simulations are complex (i.e., have many parameters), and it is often a simple exercise to cause them to reproduce system behavior by adjusting mechanisms or parameter values (e.g., von Dassow et al, 2000). Second, there are few, if any, biological systems for which species and reactions are understood in sufficient detail to constrain their simulations. In general, for well-studied biological processes we do not know the amount of each of the molecular species and reaction rates or rate-surrogates (e.g., disassociation constant, probability of reaction after collision). Third, it is difficult for researchers to interact constructively with such simulations (100+ dimension parameter and variable spaces are common). Fourth, there are significant computational challenges, including handling the large numbers of related molecular species and overall computational cost. As we will describe below, we believe that all of these problems can be addressed and that the time is right to do so for a tractable biological system.

We highlight four lines of work germane to the project. First, studies on chemotaxis in E. coli and S. typhimurium (Morton-Firth & Bray, 1998; Bray et al., 1998; Alon et al., 1999; Morton-Firth et al., 1999; Cluzel et al., 2000) demonstrate the reasonableness of representing a signal transduction network (arguably, a better characterized and somewhat simpler one) at the level of species and reactions. Second, modeling of MAP kinase cascades (including work by one of us) suggests that these systems 1) can amplify a signal and turn small input changes into decisive changes in output (Huang & Ferrell, 1996; Ferrell & Bhatt, 1997; Ferrell & Machleder, 1998) and 2) can influence signal propagation by concentrating pathway components on scaffold proteins (Levchenko et al., 2000); both suggestions are among the scientific issues that will be addressed by the Alpha Project. Third, there have been comprehensive efforts to model the entire cell cycle of S. cerevisiae (e.g., Chen et al., 2000 and references therein), the organism the Center will study. The cell cycle state impacts pheromone response, and these cell cycle models provide valuable inputs to our efforts. Fourth, the Alliance for Cellular Signaling is studying chemotaxis in B-lymphocytes and contraction and hypertrophy in cardiac myocytes (http://cellularsignalling.org). As the molecular mechanisms that underlie these processes are much less well understood than pheromone response in yeast, we do not anticipate that data from the Alliance will provide a great deal of input into our simulations. However, the Alpha Project and the Alliance are well positioned to aid one another's methods development, and our experience in modeling a signal transduction pathway may impact their subsequent efforts.
